currency.name: Euro
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 30 EUR (Northern Ireland uses pounds sterling)

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Ireland on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, on the far western edge. Ireland is an island in the Atlantic, just west of Great Britain 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Ireland?
mapQuestions.answer: Dublin, on the east coast. About 1.5 million in the wider metro area. Birthplace of Guinness, literature giants, U2, and a famously friendly pub scene.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Ireland speak?
mapQuestions.answer: English (everyone) and Irish (Gaeilge, the country's ORIGINAL language, taught in schools and a first language in some western regions called the "Gaeltacht").

mapQuestions.question: Why are there TWO Irelands?
mapQuestions.answer: The Republic of Ireland (this pack) is an independent country with its own government, dating from 1922. Northern Ireland is a separate region of the United Kingdom. The two are linked by history, geography and shared culture, but they are separate today.

mapQuestions.question: How rainy is Ireland?
mapQuestions.answer: Pretty rainy. The Atlantic westerly winds bring rain about 150 days a year on the west coast. That's why Ireland is so green. The locals joke that you can experience all four seasons in one afternoon.

foods.name: Irish stew
foods.description: Lamb (sometimes mutton or beef) slow-simmered with potatoes, onions and carrots in a clear broth. Traditional, hearty, served with thick brown soda bread.

foods.name: Soda bread
foods.description: A round dense loaf made with buttermilk and baking soda (no yeast), with a cross cut into the top "to let the devil out". Eaten warm with butter and jam.

foods.name: Full Irish breakfast
foods.description: Bacon, sausage, black and white pudding, fried egg, baked beans, grilled tomato, mushrooms and toast. A classic Sunday morning.

foods.name: Boxty
foods.description: A traditional potato pancake (mashed + grated potato, mixed and pan-fried). "Boxty in the pan, boxty in the bowl, if you can't make boxty you'll never get a man" is an old rhyme.

foods.name: Fish and chips
foods.description: Fresh haddock or cod in crispy batter with thick-cut fried chips, soaked in malt vinegar and salt. Best from a chip shop by the sea.

foods.name: Tea with biscuits
foods.description: Ireland drinks more tea per person than almost any country (Barry's or Lyons brands). With a chocolate biscuit. Builders, grandmothers, teenagers, everyone.

scavengerItems.label: A real shamrock (the three-leaved Irish national plant)
Sheep grazing on a green hillside (Ireland has more sheep than people in many counties)
A "trad" music session (fiddle, tin whistle, bodhrán drum, uillean pipes) in a pub
The Cliffs of Moher rising 214 m straight out of the Atlantic
A ruined castle or medieval round tower
A double rainbow over green fields after a passing shower
A Celtic-cross gravestone in a country churchyard
The Guinness Storehouse in Dublin (with the panoramic Gravity Bar at the top)
An Atlantic puffin (May to August) on Skellig Michael or Saltee Islands
A border collie working sheep on a farm
A High Cross or Ogham stone (ancient Irish writing)
The Wild Atlantic Way coast road winding along sea cliffs

geoMatches.description: The capital, on the east coast. Trinity College and the Book of Kells, Guinness Storehouse, Temple Bar pubs, beautiful Georgian doorways painted every colour.
A small Atlantic-coast city on the west, famous for traditional Irish music, pub sessions, oysters, and the start of the Wild Atlantic Way road trip.
Sheer Atlantic cliffs on the west coast, 214 m tall and 8 km long. Birds nesting in the cliff face, the wind sometimes strong enough to lift water from below back UP over the top.
A spectacular 179 km coastal driving loop in the south-west, around the Iveragh Peninsula. Beaches, mountains, lakes, sheep, every Irish landscape in one day.

A pyramid-shaped rocky island 12 km off the south-west coast, with 6th-century stone monks' beehive huts on top, accessible by climbing 600 stone steps. Filmed as Luke Skywalker's hideaway in Star Wars.
A 5,200-year-old burial passage tomb (older than Stonehenge and the pyramids). On the winter solstice, sunrise light enters a small window and illuminates the inner chamber.

senses.smell: Wet grass after rain, peat smoke from a country pub fire, salty Atlantic air, fresh soda bread from a kitchen, the malty smell of a Guinness brewery…
senses.hear: "What's the craic?", "Sláinte!", trad music in a pub session (fiddles, tin whistles, bodhráns), Atlantic waves on the western cliffs, sheep bleating across a hillside, light rain on a slate roof…
senses.taste: Hearty Irish stew with potato, sharp tangy farmhouse cheese, fresh oysters from Galway Bay, sweet brown soda bread with butter, hot tea with biscuits…
senses.feel: Wet wind on your face on the west coast, the warmth of a peat fire in a pub, soft Aran wool sweaters, the cold of an Atlantic beach, the smooth wood of a pub bar polished by 100 years of elbows…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Ireland so green?
stories.body: Ireland is called "the Emerald Isle" because it really IS strikingly green, more or less all year. The reason is rain: Atlantic westerlies bring moist air over the island constantly, dropping rain about 150 days a year on the west coast. The grass never stops growing, even in winter. Cows can graze outdoors most of the year. The climate also stays mild (5°C in winter, 18°C in summer) because of the warm Gulf Stream ocean current that wraps around Ireland from the Caribbean. So: lots of rain, mild temperatures, plenty of sunshine between showers. Ideal for grass. The result is the most green country in Europe.

stories.question: What is older than the pyramids?
stories.body: In County Meath, just north of Dublin, sits Newgrange: a 5,200-year-old (3,200 BC) Neolithic passage tomb. That's older than the pyramids of Egypt and older than Stonehenge. It is a low grass-covered mound 85 m across, with a stone tunnel 19 m long leading to a central burial chamber. The most extraordinary thing about Newgrange is that on the winter solstice (21 December), for about 17 minutes around sunrise, light from the rising sun travels precisely down the tunnel and illuminates the inner chamber. The builders aligned the entire structure to catch the shortest day of the year, 5,200 years ago. Visiting Newgrange feels like visiting science fiction from the past.
stories.thinkingQuestion: 5,200 years ago people built a tomb that lights up only one morning a year. What does that say about what mattered to them?

stories.question: What is a "trad session"?
stories.body: In Irish pubs on most weekend evenings (and some weeknights), musicians sit in a corner with fiddles, tin whistles, accordions, bodhrán drums, and uillean pipes, and play traditional Irish music together. This is called a "trad session". Players bring their own instruments and sit in. The music is centuries old, sometimes faster ("jigs", "reels") sometimes slow ("airs", lament songs). Anyone can join if they know the tune. The atmosphere is informal: people chat at other tables, the music swells and falls, drinks get drunk slowly, sometimes one player sings a verse and then nods to another musician to take over. Trad sessions are one of the most genuine cultural experiences in Ireland.

stories.question: Why are the Atlantic cliffs so dramatic?
stories.body: On Ireland's west coast, the land ends in 214-metre cliffs that drop straight into the Atlantic Ocean. The Cliffs of Moher are made of layered shale and sandstone, slowly being eaten away by waves. Atlantic storms can be so powerful here that waves throw water up the entire face and right over the top of the cliff. A million people a year visit. The cliffs are home to thousands of nesting seabirds: puffins, guillemots, kittiwakes, fulmars and razorbills. The Wild Atlantic Way coastal road runs for 2,500 km along Ireland's west coast, with cliffs, beaches, fishing villages and surf breaks all along the way. The Cliffs of Moher are the dramatic centrepiece.

stories.question: Why are there two Irelands?
stories.body: For most of history, Ireland was one island. After centuries of difficult English-Irish history, in 1921 the British government and Irish leaders agreed to a partition: the southern 26 counties became the independent "Irish Free State" (later the Republic of Ireland), while the 6 north-eastern counties stayed part of the United Kingdom as Northern Ireland. The 20th century brought decades of tension, especially "the Troubles" (roughly 1968 to 1998) in Northern Ireland between communities. The 1998 Good Friday Agreement brought peace and an open border between the two parts of the island. Today, you can cross between the Republic and Northern Ireland on a road with no checkpoints. Both sides share music, language, sport, history and a complicated, hard-earned peace.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Ireland so far?
Have you tried saying "go raibh maith agat" yet?
Dublin, the west coast, mountains, ruins, which has felt the most "Ireland"?
Did you go to a trad music session? Did you stay for the whole night?
How does Irish weather change how the country feels?
Newgrange was built 5,200 years ago to catch one sunrise a year. What does that tell you about ancient people?

wordSearch.hint: Irish foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Sheep
animals.description: Ireland has around 4 million sheep across 130,000 farms. Black-faced mountain sheep, white Suffolks, Connemara breeds.

animals.name: Dairy cow
animals.description: Friesian and Holstein dairy cows in every Irish field. Irish dairy (especially butter) is famous worldwide.

animals.name: Atlantic puffin
animals.description: Half-pigeon, half-clown. Nests on western islands like Skellig Michael, Saltees, the Cliffs of Moher (May-August).

animals.name: White-tailed eagle
animals.description: Reintroduced to Killarney National Park after going extinct in Ireland. Now breeding again, with a 2.5 m wingspan.

animals.name: Common dolphin
animals.description: Plays in the bow waves off the west coast of Ireland, especially around the Dingle Peninsula.

animals.name: Otter
animals.description: Found in clean Irish rivers and along the west coast in sea estuaries.

animals.name: Connemara pony
animals.description: A small, sturdy native Irish pony breed from the wild west coast. Often grey or dun.
